video_title: A 6 Standard Deviation Tech Event Just Happened. Now What? | WDWL?
gist: Data Trek's Nick Kohus and Jessica Rae argue US large-cap tech just posted a 6-standard-deviation 50-day outperformance vs the S&P (29.3 ppts through June 2), an unprecedented statistical extreme that argues for caution and rotation out of semis even as the broader S&P (only ~2 sigma) likely still has room to run. They also break down the unusual fixed-price $135/share, $1.8T SpaceX IPO as a real-time gauge of where we sit versus 1999, and conclude today is an echo of the dot-com era but nowhere near its 84% six-month NASDAQ blow-off.

insights.claim: US large-cap tech (XLK) outperformed the S&P 500 by 29.3 percentage points over the prior 50 days as of June 2, a six-standard-deviation event versus a decade-long mean outperformance of 1.6 ppts and standard deviation of 4 ppts, the most extreme reading in Data Trek's dataset by a wide margin.
insights.speaker: Jessica Rae, co-founder Data Trek Research
insights.so_what: An unprecedented statistical extreme is a flashing caution signal for tech exposure; mean-reversion risk is elevated even if direction is unknowable.
insights.quote: tech just beat the S&P by 29 percentage points over the prior 50 days on June 2nd. That's over a six standard deviation event and the most extreme reading in our data set by a wide margin.

insights.claim: The 50-day window that produced the 6-sigma tech move started six days before the late-March 2025 lows, so the extreme reading is inclusive of the year's biggest selloff and dwarfs every prior stress-and-recovery episode including the 2020 pandemic, 2022 rate shock, 2025 trade-policy shock, and repeated AI sentiment scares.
insights.speaker: Jessica Rae, co-founder Data Trek Research
insights.so_what: There is no historical playbook for unwinding a move this large, so position sizing in tech should assume genuinely uncharted tail risk.
insights.quote: This move dwarfs every prior stress and recovery period recovery episode, including the 2020 pandemic crisis, 2022's rate shock, 2025's trade policy shock, and repeated AI sentiment scares.

insights.claim: Despite the statistical extreme, Data Trek remains long-term bullish on tech but explicitly cautious here, framing it as 'okay to keep playing, but keep looking for the next car' rather than a green-light 'game on' moment.
insights.speaker: Nick Kohus / Jessica Rae, Data Trek Research
insights.so_what: Signals a bullish-but-de-risking stance: stay invested in the AI theme while trimming and watching for the reversal trigger.
insights.quote: we're we're long-term we've always been long-term bulls on on tech, but six standard deviations is just absolutely off the charts.

insights.claim: US large-cap semiconductors (SMH) beat software (IGV) by 44 percentage points over the prior 50 days as of June 3, four standard deviations above the long-run 2.7-ppt semi outperformance mean and the most extreme reading in a decade.
insights.speaker: Jessica Rae, co-founder Data Trek Research
insights.so_what: A semi-vs-software spread this stretched flags semis as the most reversion-prone sub-group; tactical investors should consider trimming semis for software.
insights.quote: as of June 3rd, semis have beaten software by 44 points. That's four standard deviations above the long run mean.

insights.claim: Semiconductors structurally outperform software over time because, even with Moore's Law slowing, semiconductor compute per dollar still roughly doubles fairly quickly, letting chip companies leverage products across an ever-increasing range of use cases.
insights.speaker: Jessica Rae, co-founder Data Trek Research
insights.so_what: Explains why the semi-over-software baseline edge is real and widening, so reversion is a tactical pause, not a thesis break.
insights.quote: semicmputee per dollar still doubles fairly quickly, and that allows these companies to leverage your products for ever for an everinccreasing range of use cases.

insights.claim: Data Trek recommends tactical investors reduce semi exposure and look at software, while longer-term AI believers adopt an index-weight approach to the whole tech sector to keep theme exposure while cutting the concentration risk a semi-reversion would disproportionately punish.
insights.speaker: Jessica Rae, co-founder Data Trek Research
insights.so_what: Concrete portfolio action: rotate from concentrated semi bets toward broad tech / software to hedge mean-reversion.
insights.quote: for tactical investors that argues for reducing semi-exposure and looking more closely at software here.

insights.claim: The recent acceleration in tech's outperformance came not from rotation out of semis but from software names rallying alongside semis, with Oracle, CrowdStrike and Palo Alto surging while Microsoft lagged, turning what had been a software drag into a broad simultaneous tech rally before Friday's selloff.
insights.speaker: host (Josh Brown)
insights.so_what: Breadth within tech (software + cyber joining semis) was the marginal accelerant; watch whether that breadth holds or unwinds.
insights.quote: all of a sudden this drag on tech sort of reversed itself. And then it was like, oh wait a minute, this is turning into a tech rally for the ages.

themes.theme: S&P Breadth as the Bull Case

insights.claim: Unlike tech, the broad S&P 500 was up 15.3% over the prior 50 days as of June 1, only a two-standard-deviation move versus its 2.4% decade average, signaling strength without tech's extreme excess.
insights.speaker: Jessica Rae, co-founder Data Trek Research
insights.so_what: The index itself is not over-extended, so a tech pullback need not mean a broad-market top.
insights.quote: as of June 1st index was up 15.3% over the prior 50 days. That's a two standard deviation move. So still strong but not nearly as extreme.

insights.claim: In all five prior instances since 2015 when the S&P first crossed a two-sigma 50-day return threshold, the market was higher 50 trading days later, averaging a 7.3% forward return with the weakest outcome still up 2.6%.
insights.speaker: Jessica Rae, co-founder Data Trek Research
insights.so_what: Momentum-as-factor history argues the S&P still has room to run even as semis pause, supporting staying invested in the broad index.
insights.quote: in every case, the market was higher 50 trading days later. And the average forward return was 7.3%. Even the weakest outcome still gained 2.6%.

insights.claim: Market-making algorithms are explicitly built on 50- and 100-day standard deviations of daily returns, so they recognized tech's statistical extreme and widened bid-ask spreads / stepped back rather than provide liquidity, amplifying Friday's cascading selloff.
insights.speaker: Nick Kohus, co-founder Data Trek Research
insights.so_what: Algos that know the math will likely keep amplifying any further tech selling, raising the odds of sharp follow-through downside.
insights.quote: They literally are built on 50 and 100 day standard deviation of daily returns... I'm widening out my bid ass spread. I'm not going to take any risk because I see this as a rollover after a statistically very anomalous period.

insights.claim: Tech fell 6.66% Friday, but Kohus estimates it would have been down only ~3% on the semis rollover alone, with macro/rates selling on top of heavy retail ownership, leverage and options exposure exaggerating the move.
insights.speaker: Nick Kohus / Jessica Rae, Data Trek Research
insights.so_what: The eye-popping headline drop was partly mechanical/macro; the underlying tech damage was milder, but elevated retail leverage means future shocks can over-shoot.
insights.quote: tech was down 6.66% 66% which kind of was a eyepopping number for you know numerological reasons probably would have been down 3% just on the semis rollover but the macro did not help.

insights.claim: Kohus reads the selloff as originating in the US memory/semi trade (SanDisk, Micron) first and then spilling into Asia, with South Korea — up 100% and even more extreme versus EM than US tech is versus the S&P — repeatedly triggering 'sidecar' circuit-breaker trading slowdowns over the prior three sessions.
insights.speaker: Nick Kohus, co-founder Data Trek Research
insights.so_what: South Korea's even-more-stretched setup is a leading tell; its circuit-breaker frequency signals where global semi stress could next erupt.
insights.quote: South Korea is up 100%. And it looks even crazier versus em than tech does versus the S&P.

themes.theme: Rates as the New Risk to Tech

insights.claim: Friday's jobs report and revisions to the prior two months reshaped the US labor market from slowing to fairly healthy, pushing the 2-year yield to a new 12-month high and reviving fears of a Fed rate hike and policy mistake that exacerbated the tech selloff.
insights.speaker: Nick Kohus, co-founder Data Trek Research
insights.so_what: Rates re-entered as a tech risk factor; a 2-year breakout to new highs is the signal to watch for further pressure on long-duration tech.
insights.quote: two years hit a new 12-month high and you know the the worries about a Fed rate hike and potential Fed policy mistakes and higher cost of capital that didn't help either.

insights.claim: Data Trek argues higher rates only threaten tech if they rise fast enough to create a rational recession fear, since hyperscalers ultimately have cyclical cash flows — the same dynamic that hit tech in 2022.
insights.speaker: Jessica Rae, co-founder Data Trek Research
insights.so_what: Frames the rate trigger: watch the speed/rate-of-change of yields, not the absolute level, as the tech-risk threshold.
insights.quote: higher rates are only an issue if they go up so quickly that there's a rational fear of recession.

insights.claim: Rate-cut expectations have fully reversed — six months ago insurance cuts were discussed, now no one is, and Goldman Sachs has removed any rate cut from its year-end forecast — with the June 16-17 FOMC being the first meeting under a Kevin Warsh-led Fed that tech investors are watching closely.
insights.speaker: Jessica Rae, co-founder Data Trek Research
insights.so_what: A hawkish regime shift plus a new Fed chair is a key catalyst window; the June FOMC could reprice tech's rate sensitivity.
insights.quote: Goldman Sachs just removed any interest rate cut from its year-end forecast.

insights.claim: Cycle-high 2-year yields have historically preceded recessions by about one to two years, so a fresh breakout to new highs in twos late in the cycle reintroduces recession risk to the outlook.
insights.speaker: Nick Kohus, co-founder Data Trek Research
insights.so_what: A 2-year yield breakout is a recession-clock signal; investors should treat new cycle highs in twos as a late-cycle warning.
insights.quote: you get a recession about a year or two after a cycle high in twos. So when you break out to a new high, you start thinking, uh, now I've got to worry about a recession again.

insights.claim: SpaceX is set to go public possibly at the end of the week as an unusual fixed-price issuance at $135/share and a ~$1.8 trillion valuation, bypassing traditional book-building so that institutions are forced to take the dictated price and retail effectively sets the day-one valuation.
insights.speaker: Nick Kohus, co-founder Data Trek Research
insights.so_what: Price discovery happens only on the first trade, so day-one action — not the deal terms — is the real valuation signal to watch.
insights.quote: Price discovery is not happening in this IPO. Price discovery is happening on the first trade and thereafter

insights.claim: Kohus frames SpaceX's first-day close as a real-time read on where the market sits versus history: a $154-166 close implies an average 1997-98 (14-22%) tech IPO, $174 matches last year's average, and a close near $231 (a ~71% pop, the 1999 average) would mean we are 'legit in a 99 bubble.'
insights.speaker: Nick Kohus, co-founder Data Trek Research
insights.so_what: Gives a precise day-one scorecard ($135 base, ~$231 = bubble) to calibrate overall market froth from a single print on Friday.
insights.quote: if if if SpaceX closes day 1 231, we are legit in a 99 bubble, particularly with a deal of this size.

insights.claim: SpaceX coming public at ~$1.8 trillion gives Tesla its first real valuation comp (Tesla traded ~$1.5T Friday); Kohus frames Tesla as a near-dated Musk option (24-36 months on robots/free cash flow) and SpaceX as a 5-15 year long-dated Musk bet, arguing investors should own 'both or none.'
insights.speaker: Nick Kohus, co-founder Data Trek Research
insights.so_what: Reframes Tesla/SpaceX as duration-differentiated bets on Musk; a SpaceX comp could anchor Tesla's valuation debate.
insights.quote: Tesla to my mind is like a near dated Musk option... SpaceX is a very longdated version of Musk. It's 5, 10, 15 years.

insights.claim: SpaceX faces fast-entry NASDAQ-100/QQQ inclusion ~15 days after IPO adding roughly $15-20B of demand on a $75B float (~20%), but unusually short lockups (20% of non-Musk stock frees right after Q2 earnings, more after Q3) will create heavy supply and elevated volatility in the first weeks.
insights.speaker: Nick Kohus, co-founder Data Trek Research
insights.so_what: Index-demand vs early-unlock supply is a tradeable tension; expect volatility as the two forces fight over the first few weeks.
insights.quote: 20% of the stock X Musk stake comes available right after Q2 earnings... So there's a lot more stock coming than just the usual IPO lock up a lot more.

insights.claim: Given SpaceX's large size and heavy retail demand, there is real risk of a day-one market-structure hiccup on the NASDAQ, echoing the 2012 Facebook IPO (order-fill confusion plus selective mobile-traction disclosure drove it from 42 to ~12) and the August 2015 ETF flash crash.
insights.speaker: Nick Kohus / host (Josh Brown)
insights.so_what: Operational/plumbing risk on listing day is a discrete hazard; traders should expect possible fill delays and dislocations Friday.
insights.quote: huge volume sometimes overwhelms the system and let's just be on the watch out for that.

insights.claim: From the start of Q4 1999 to the March 10, 2000 top the NASDAQ rose 84% (up 37% in Q4 then another 24% into March), then fell 34% from March 10 to April 14 — volatility far exceeding anything in today's tech move including Friday.
insights.speaker: Nick Kohus, co-founder Data Trek Research
insights.so_what: Sets a quantitative bar for a true bubble (an ~80% melt-up + ~30% crash) that today's market is nowhere near, tempering 1999 panic.
insights.quote: from the beginning of Q499 to the top in March 2000, the NASDAQ was up 84%. Almost a double in less than half a year.

insights.claim: Kohus's bubble test is a 50%-up-then-30%-down round trip in tech, and by the 'a double is a bubble' rule the NASDAQ Composite up only ~34% over the last year is nowhere close, so today is an echo of 1999 but the extremes aren't extreme enough.
insights.speaker: Nick Kohus, co-founder Data Trek Research
insights.so_what: Provides a clear yardstick for declaring a bubble; current readings say not yet, supporting staying invested.
insights.quote: a double is a bubble and the NASDAQ composite that happened at the height of the dot bubble were up like 34% over the last year nowhere close.

insights.claim: Brown argues an 84% six-month NASDAQ-100 melt-up is now structurally near-impossible because the dollar market caps of the largest companies are too large — 'I don't think the dollars exist for that' — though bubble dynamics can still appear in small corners like quantum stocks.
insights.speaker: host (Josh Brown)
insights.so_what: Implies index-level 1999-style blow-offs are unlikely; froth and bubble risk should be hunted in niche/small-cap pockets instead.
insights.quote: to get that in the modern NASDAQ 100 and 84% six-month rally, I almost don't think you can because the dollar amounts of the largest market cap companies are too large.
